name: Kingdom of Elam
note: One of the oldest civilisations in Iran, centred on Susa. Rivalled Mesopotamian powers for millennia. The ziggurat of Chogha Zanbil (c.1250 BCE) is a UNESCO World Heritage Site. Elamite sacked Babylon and captured the Code of Hammurabi. Absorbed into the Achaemenid Empire.

name: Hittite Empire
note: The dominant Anatolian power of the Bronze Age. Their sudden collapse (~1180 BC) during the Bronze Age Collapse remains one of history's great mysteries.

name: Mitanni Empire
note: Hurrian-speaking empire in northern Mesopotamia. The ruling elite used Indo-Aryan names and worshipped Vedic deities (Mitra, Varuna, Indra) — evidence of early Indo-Aryan migration. Major rival of Egypt and the Hittites. Destroyed by the Assyrians.

name: Olmec Civilization
note: Mother culture of Mesoamerica

name: Kingdom of Saba (Sheba)
note: Ancient South Arabian kingdom — the Biblical Queen of Sheba's realm. Controlled the lucrative frankincense and myrrh trade. Built the Great Dam of Ma'rib, one of the engineering wonders of the ancient world. The dam's final collapse (c.570 CE) is mentioned in the Quran.

name: Zhou Dynasty
note: Late Zhou (Warring States) produced Confucius, Laozi, Mencius, Han Fei — the foundational philosophical schools that would define all subsequent Chinese governance

name: Kingdom of Urartu
note: The Iron Age kingdom centered on Lake Van (modern eastern Turkey/Armenia). Urartu was a major rival of the Neo-Assyrian Empire for control of the Armenian Highlands. Known for impressive metalwork (Urartian bronzes) and large-scale irrigation systems. Destroyed by the Scythian/Median coalition around 590 BCE. The Armenian people are their cultural successors.

name: Carthage (Phoenician City-State)
note: Founded by Phoenicians from Tyre. Dominated western Mediterranean trade. The three Punic Wars against Rome (264–146 BCE) were among the most consequential conflicts in ancient history. Hannibal Barca's crossing of the Alps (218 BCE) nearly destroyed Rome. Carthage was razed in 146 BCE; later refounded as a Roman colony.

name: Kingdom of Meroe (Meroitic Kingdom)
note: After the Kushite 25th Dynasty was expelled from Egypt by the Assyrians (-656), Nubian power retreated south to Meroe on the Nile. Meroe developed a distinct civilisation with its own undeciphered script (Meroitic), iron smelting industry, and distinctive pyramid style (steeper than Egyptian). Ruled by queen-regents (Kandakes) alongside or instead of kings. Fell to the Aksumite kingdom of Ethiopia around 350 CE.

name: Neo-Babylonian (Chaldean) Empire
note: Nebuchadnezzar II; Chaldean dynasty deliberately revived ancient Babylonian identity

name: Achaemenid Empire
note: Used Aramaic as the administrative lingua franca but Persian was the dynastic identity; Cyrus was notably religiously tolerant

name: Tupí-Guaraní Peoples
note: Coastal and Amazonian Brazil

name: Zapotec (Monte Albán)
note: Oaxacan civilization

name: Nanda Empire
note: The Nanda dynasty united most of northern India under Mahapadma Nanda. Enormous army reportedly of 200,000 infantry. Overthrown by Chandragupta Maurya with the guidance of Chanakya (Kautilya).

name: Maurya Empire
note: Ashoka converted to Buddhism and propagated it across Asia — a major religious shift mid-dynasty that changed the empire's entire ideological character

name: Ptolemaic Kingdom
note: Greek-speaking Macedonian rulers adopted Egyptian royal titles and iconography for legitimacy, but the court was Hellenic

name: Chera Dynasty
note: One of the three crowned Tamil kings of the Sangam period alongside the Cholas and Pandyas. Controlled Kerala and western Tamil Nadu. The later Chera Perumals of Mahodayapuram (9th-12th c.) patronised both Hinduism and Buddhism.

name: Chola Empire
note: One of the longest-ruling dynasties in world history. The medieval Chola Empire under Rajaraja I and Rajendra I (985–1044) built a maritime empire spanning Sri Lanka, the Maldives, and Southeast Asia. The Chola navy was one of the most powerful in the Indian Ocean. Temples at Thanjavur and Gangaikondacholapuram are UNESCO World Heritage Sites.

name: Pandya Dynasty
note: Ancient Tamil dynasty centred on Madurai. One of the three crowned kings of the Sangam period alongside the Cholas and Cheras. Marco Polo visited the Pandya kingdom in the 13th century. Defeated by the Delhi Sultanate's Malik Kafur expedition (1311) and the Madurai Sultanate.

name: Greco-Bactrian Kingdom
note: Hellenistic successor state in Central Asia. Founded when Diodotus I declared independence from the Seleucids. Controlled Bactria and at times parts of Sogdiana and Gandhara. Remarkable fusion of Greek and Central Asian culture. Fell to Yuezhi/Saka invasions.

name: Parthian Empire (Arsacid)
note: Highly decentralized; Hellenized early, then reverted to Iranian/Zoroastrian identity

name: Satavahana Empire
note: First great Deccan empire; established the bridge between northern Aryan and southern Dravidian cultures. Patronised both Hinduism and Buddhism. Their trade networks stretched to Rome.

name: Qin Dynasty
note: State philosophy was Legalism — entirely different from Confucianism. Qin's brutal efficiency unified China but was ideologically rejected by every subsequent dynasty

name: Han Dynasty
note: Established Confucianism as state ideology and the civil examination system — the template all later Chinese dynasties followed

name: Kingdom of Numidia
note: Berber kingdom in North Africa (modern Algeria). Massinissa unified Numidia as a Roman ally against Carthage. His descendant Jugurtha fought the Jugurthine War against Rome (112–106 BCE). Julius Caesar annexed Numidia after defeating the Pompeians at Thapsus (46 BCE).

name: Indo-Scythian Kingdom
note: Saka (Scythian) rulers who displaced the Indo-Greeks. The Western Satraps and Northern Satraps were the major branches. Patronised Buddhism and used Greek-derived scripts. Azes I established the Azes era (c.47 BCE) used in dating.

name: Kingdom of Armenia
note: The Artaxiad (Artashesian) dynasty founded the first unified Armenian state after Antiochus III's defeat by Rome. Tigranes II briefly created an Armenian empire stretching from Caspian to Mediterranean. Armenia was the first nation to adopt Christianity as state religion (301 CE). The kingdom oscillated between Roman and Parthian/Sassanid spheres until partitioned in 428.

name: Shunga Dynasty
note: Founded by Pushyamitra Shunga after assassinating the last Maurya emperor. Patronised Brahmanical Hinduism and Sanskrit literature. Built the great stupa at Bharhut. Defended against Indo-Greek incursions from the northwest.

name: Indo-Greek Kingdom
note: Hellenistic kingdoms in northwestern India and Pakistan. Menander I (Milinda) was the most famous ruler — the Buddhist text Milinda Panha records his dialogues with the monk Nagasena. The Indo-Greeks introduced Hellenistic art to South Asia, foundational to Gandhara Buddhist art.

name: Hasmonean Dynasty
note: Jewish priestly dynasty that won independence from the Seleucids through the Maccabean Revolt (167–160 BCE). Established the last independent Jewish state before modern Israel. Internal civil war invited Roman intervention (63 BCE) leading to eventual replacement by the Herodian dynasty.

name: Nabataean Kingdom
note: The Nabataeans were Arab traders who built Petra as their capital in the Jordanian desert — one of antiquity's great architectural achievements (the 'rose-red city'). They controlled the spice trade routes between Arabia, Egypt, and the Mediterranean. Annexed by the Roman Emperor Trajan as the province Arabia Petraea in 106 CE. Their distinctive Aramaic script evolved into modern Arabic script.

name: Himyarite Kingdom
note: Last great pre-Islamic South Arabian kingdom. Uniquely adopted Judaism as state religion under King Dhu Nuwas. His persecution of Christians at Najran (523 CE) provoked an Ethiopian (Aksumite) invasion that ended Himyarite rule and paved the way for Islam.

name: Teotihuacan
note: Classic-era Mesoamerican metropolis

name: Dacian Kingdom
note: Powerful kingdom in modern Romania. Burebista briefly united all Dacian tribes (82–44 BCE). Decebalus fought two major wars against Trajan (101–106 CE). Trajan's conquest and the subsequent Roman colonisation of Dacia is the origin myth of the Romanian people.

name: Indo-Parthian Kingdom
note: Founded by Gondophares I in the Indus region. The Apostle Thomas reportedly visited Gondophares' court. Displaced the Indo-Scythians in parts of the northwest. Absorbed by the Kushan Empire.

name: Kushan Empire
note: Founded by Kujula Kadphises from the Yuezhi confederation. Kanishka I (c.127–150 CE) convened the Fourth Buddhist Council and patronised Gandhara art — the first anthropomorphic depictions of the Buddha. Controlled trade routes connecting Rome, Persia, China, and India. The empire was a crucible of Hellenistic, Iranian, Indian, and Central Asian cultures.

name: Goguryeo
note: One of the Three Kingdoms of Korea; at its peak controlled northern Manchuria and the Korean peninsula north of the Han River. Repelled three massive Sui Chinese invasions (612–614) — the failure of those campaigns contributed to the Sui Dynasty's collapse. Finally destroyed by a Tang–Silla alliance in 668. Goguryeo identity is claimed by both modern Korea and China (as part of Gaogouli history).

name: Aksumite Kingdom
note: East African trading empire centered in modern Ethiopia/Eritrea. One of the first states to adopt Christianity (c.330 CE under King Ezana). Controlled Red Sea trade between Rome and India. Conquered the Kingdom of Kush. Mani listed it among the four great empires of his time alongside Rome, Persia, and China.

name: Ancestral Puebloans
note: Mesa Verde and Chaco Canyon builders

name: Sassanid Empire
note: Zoroastrianism as explicit state religion; Magi priestly class wielded significant power

name: Kushano-Sasanian Kingdom
note: Sasanian vassal state established after the Sasanids conquered the western Kushan territories. Used the title 'Kushanshah'. Mixed Kushan and Sasanian administrative traditions. Eventually displaced by the Kidarites.

name: Maya City-States (Tikal, Calakmul)
note: Classic Maya period

name: Vakataka Dynasty
note: Major Deccan dynasty allied by marriage with the Gupta Empire. Pravarasena II commissioned the Ajanta cave paintings — among the greatest masterworks of Buddhist art. Controlled the Vidarbha region of central India.

name: Yamato Period
note: The emergence of the Yamato imperial line and the introduction of Buddhism and Chinese characters. Includes the Kofun and Asuka sub-periods.

name: Palmyrene Empire
note: Queen Zenobia's breakaway empire that briefly controlled Syria, Egypt, and much of Anatolia. Palmyra was a fabulously wealthy caravan city linking Rome and Persia. Defeated by the Roman Emperor Aurelian (272–273). Zenobia was paraded in golden chains through Rome.

name: Pallava Dynasty
note: Major South Indian dynasty centred on Kanchipuram. Pioneered Dravidian temple architecture — the Shore Temple and rathas at Mahabalipuram are their masterwork. Narasimhavarman I defeated the Chalukya Pulakeshin II. Patrons of Sanskrit and Tamil literature.

name: Lakhmid Kingdom
note: Arab client kingdom of the Sassanid Empire based at al-Hira (Iraq). Predominantly Nestorian Christian. A buffer state and cultural bridge between Persia and Arabia. The Lakhmid court patronised pre-Islamic Arabic poetry. Abolished by the Sassanid Khosrow II.

name: Gupta Empire
note: Hindu 'Golden Age'; Vaishnavism as court religion; classical Sanskrit literature and mathematics flourished

name: Kingdom of Makuria
note: Christian Nubian kingdom centred on Dongola. Defeated an Arab invasion at the Battle of Dongola (652), forcing the Baqt treaty — one of the longest-lasting peace agreements in history (~600 years). The Faras Cathedral murals are masterworks of medieval Christian art.

name: Kadamba Dynasty
note: First indigenous dynasty to rule Karnataka. Founded by Mayurasharma. Established Banavasi as capital. Pioneered the use of Kannada as an administrative language. Later became feudatories of the Chalukyas.

name: Hunnic Empire
note: Attila's confederation devastated the Roman Empire and triggered the Great Migration. The Huns are of uncertain ethnic origin (possibly Turkic, possibly mixed). Their western empire collapsed immediately after Attila's death (453).

name: Kamarupa Kingdom
note: Ancient kingdom in Assam and northeastern India. Bhaskaravarman was an ally of Harsha. Xuanzang visited and described it as a prosperous Buddhist centre. The kingdom's Pragjyotishpura (Guwahati) was a renowned centre of Tantric learning.

name: Byzantine Empire
note: Eastern Orthodox Christianity is inseparable from Byzantine identity; emperor as head of both church and state

name: Visigothic Kingdom
note: The Visigoths settled in southwestern Gaul (Aquitaine) as foederati in 418; after defeat by Clovis at Vouillé (507) they retreated to Iberia, making Toledo their capital. Converted from Arianism to Catholicism in 589 under Reccared — the defining event that integrated Visigothic and Hispano-Roman populations. Destroyed by the Muslim Umayyad invasion of 711.

name: Vandal Kingdom of North Africa
note: The Vandals crossed into North Africa in 429 under Gaiseric, having previously swept through Spain. They captured Carthage in 439, making it their capital. The Vandal fleet dominated the western Mediterranean and sacked Rome in 455. 'Vandalism' as a concept derives from their reputation, though they were no more destructive than other Germanic peoples. Destroyed by the Byzantine general Belisarius in 533 in a remarkably swift campaign.

name: Hephthalite Empire
note: The 'White Huns' who dominated Central and South Asia. Mihirakula was their most feared ruler in India. Defeated the Gupta Empire and the Sasanids. Destroyed by a Sasanid-Göktürk alliance c.560. Left lasting impact on the ethnic composition of Afghanistan and Pakistan.

name: Kingdom of Odoacer
note: Odoacer (Odovacer), a Germanic chieftain of Scirian/Hunnic origin, deposed the last Western Roman Emperor Romulus Augustulus on September 4, 476 — the conventional end-date of the Western Roman Empire. He ruled as King of Italy under nominal Eastern Roman suzerainty, maintaining Roman administrative structures. Defeated and killed by Theodoric the Ostrogoth at a peace banquet in Ravenna (493).

name: Frankish Kingdom / Carolingian Empire
note: Clovis I unified the Franks (486) and converted to Catholic Christianity, giving the Franks papal legitimacy. Charlemagne (768–814) conquered the Saxons, Lombards, and Avars; crowned Emperor by Pope Leo III on Christmas 800. The first attempt at a unified Western European empire since Rome. Treaty of Verdun (843) split the empire into West Francia (future France), East Francia (future Germany), and Middle Francia.

name: Ostrogothic Kingdom of Italy
note: Theodoric the Great established the most sophisticated post-Roman Germanic kingdom — a genuine synthesis of Gothic and Roman culture. He ruled through the existing Roman administrative machinery while keeping Goths as the military class. His court at Ravenna patronised art and philosophy (Boethius wrote the Consolation of Philosophy in his prison). Destroyed by the Byzantine reconquest under Justinian I (Gothic Wars 535–554).

name: Chalukya Dynasty (Badami)
note: The Badami Chalukyas were the first great Deccan empire. Pulakeshin II defeated Harsha's northward expansion. The dynasty split into the Western and Eastern Chalukyas. Rock-cut cave temples at Badami and Aihole represent early Dravidian and Nagara architecture.

name: Tiwanaku
note: Andean highland civilization

name: Avar Khaganate
note: Avars (steppe nomads of uncertain Mongolian/Turkic origin) conquered the Carpathian basin in 568 after the Lombards left for Italy. They raided Byzantium and the Frankish kingdoms repeatedly. Charlemagne destroyed the Avar state in a series of campaigns (791-796) and dismantled their treasury ('the Ring').

name: Kingdom of the Lombards
note: The Lombards (Langobards) invaded northern Italy in 568, exploiting the devastation left by the Gothic Wars. Unlike the Ostrogoths, they did not attempt to preserve Roman structures — their conquest was destructive and their integration slow. By the 8th century they had converted from Arianism to Catholicism and were partially Romanised. Pope Stephen II invited Charlemagne's father Pippin III, then Charlemagne himself (773–774), to defeat the Lombards; Charlemagne dissolved the kingdom and added 'King of the Lombards' to his titles.

name: Sui Dynasty
note: Buddhism was a major state religion alongside Confucianism; Sui rulers were devout Buddhists

name: Muisca Confederation
note: Colombian highland chiefdoms

name: Wari Empire
note: First Andean empire

name: Harsha Empire
note: Last major Hindu-Buddhist empire of the Ganges plain before the Islamic conquests. Harsha personally wrote Sanskrit plays and patronised the scholar Xuanzang (the Chinese pilgrim whose journey inspired Journey to the West). His empire collapsed immediately after his death, leaving no successors.

name: Tang Dynasty
note: Tang royal family had partial Xianbei ancestry but identified as Han; cosmopolitan — Buddhism, Taoism, Nestorian Christianity, and Islam all coexisted; Confucian exam system perfected

name: Tibetan Empire
note: Founded by Songtsen Gampo who unified the Tibetan plateau. Adopted Buddhism and created the Tibetan script. Trisong Detsen expanded the empire to its greatest extent — sacking the Tang capital Chang'an in 763 CE. One of the most powerful empires of 7th–9th century Asia. Collapsed after the assassination of Lang Darma (842), fragmenting Tibet for four centuries.

name: Khazar Khaganate
note: Ruling class converted to Judaism — a unique case; possibly a diplomatic choice to remain neutral between the Christian Byzantine and Muslim Abbasid spheres

name: Umayyad Caliphate
note: Aggressively Arab-supremacist; non-Arab Muslims (mawali) had second-class status — this fueled the Abbasid revolution

name: Unified Silla
note: Silla allied with the Tang Dynasty to defeat Baekje and Goguryeo, then successfully expelled Tang forces to unify the peninsula south of the Taedong River.

name: First Bulgarian Empire
note: Founded by Bulgars (a Turkic/steppe people) who subjugated Slavic tribes south of the Danube; the ruling Bulgar class was rapidly absorbed by the Slavic majority. Christianised under Boris I (864) who chose Constantinople over Rome — defining Bulgaria as Orthodox. The Golden Age under Simeon I (893–927) saw Bulgaria become the dominant Balkan power, rivalling Byzantium. Destroyed by Byzantine Emperor Basil II 'the Bulgar-Slayer' at the Battle of Kleidion (1014); he blinded 15,000 prisoners.

name: Balhae (Bohai)
note: Founded by Dae Joyeong after the fall of Goguryeo. Controlled most of Manchuria and northern Korean peninsula. Known as the 'Flourishing Land in the East'. Destroyed by the Khitan Liao dynasty in 926. Claimed as a successor state to Goguryeo by Korean historiography; Chinese historiography treats it as a regional regime (Bohai).

name: Al-Andalus
note: The Muslim-ruled territory of Iberia from the Umayyad conquest (711) to the fall of Granada (1492). At its peak (8th–10th centuries) Al-Andalus was the most sophisticated civilisation in Europe — Córdoba rivalled Constantinople and Baghdad. The Caliphate of Córdoba (929–1031) under Abd ar-Rahman III was the golden age. After 1031 it fragmented into taifa kingdoms; the Almoravid (1086) and Almohad (1147) Berber dynasties revived Muslim power but with more rigid Islamic orthodoxy. The Christian Reconquista progressively reduced Al-Andalus until only Granada remained (1238–1492). Concurrent with Christian kingdoms in the north throughout.

name: Pratihara Dynasty
note: The Gurjara-Pratiharas were the most powerful dynasty of early medieval North India. Nagabhata I repelled the Arab invasions of Sindh. Controlled Kannauj and dominated the Tripartite Struggle against the Rashtrakutas and Palas. Their collapse fragmented North India into Rajput kingdoms.

name: Uyghur Khaganate
note: One of history's few Manichaean states; adopted Manichaeism as state religion after 762 — a major civilizational break from Göktürk Tengriism

name: Abbasid Caliphate
note: Ruling dynasty remained Arab (Qurayshi lineage); Arabic as the language of religion and law. Persian bureaucrats (Barmakid viziers) dominated administration creating a Persianate court overlay — but the legitimacy anchor was Arab/Islamic, not Persian

name: Pala Empire
note: The last great Buddhist empire of India; patrons of Nalanda and Vikramashila universities — the apex of Indian Buddhist scholarship. Their fall to the Ghurids (1203) and the subsequent destruction of Nalanda (1203) effectively ended institutional Buddhism in India.

name: Rashtrakuta Dynasty
note: One of the most powerful Indian empires. The Kailasa temple at Ellora — carved from a single rock — is their greatest monument. Arab traveller Sulaiman called the Rashtrakuta king one of the four great kings of the world. Dominated the Tripartite Struggle for Kannauj.

name: Rustamid Dynasty
note: Ibadi Muslim dynasty in central Algeria (Tahert). One of the few Ibadi states in history. A haven of religious tolerance where Ibadi, Sunni, Shia, Christian, and Jewish communities coexisted. Destroyed by the Fatimid conquest.

name: Idrisid Dynasty
note: Founded by Idris I, a descendant of Ali ibn Abi Talib who fled the Abbasid massacre. The first Muslim dynasty of Morocco. Founded the city of Fez (808), which became one of the great centres of Islamic learning. Introduced Islam deeply into Berber North Africa.

name: Aghlabid Dynasty
note: Abbasid governors who became de facto independent in Ifriqiya (Tunisia). Conquered Sicily (827–902), bringing Islam to southern Europe. Built the Great Mosque of Kairouan. Overthrown by the Fatimid Shia revolution.

name: Mississippian Culture
note: Cahokia-centered mound-building civilization

name: Samanid Empire
note: The Samanids were the first Persian dynasty to rule independently after the Arab conquest — an Iranian Renaissance. Under their patronage, Bukhara became one of the Islamic world's great centres of learning. They championed New Persian (Dari) as a literary language: Rudaki wrote there, and Ferdowsi began his Shahnameh at their court. The Samanid bureaucratic model became the template for subsequent Persian and Turkic administrations. Destroyed by the Ghaznavids (east) and Qarakhanids (north).

name: Kara-Khanid Khanate
note: First major Turkic dynasty to convert to Islam (c.960). Conquered the Samanid Empire. Kashgari's Divan-i Lugat-it-Turk (1072–1074) — the first comprehensive dictionary of Turkic languages — was written under Kara-Khanid patronage. Yusuf Balasaguni's Kutadgu Bilig is another masterwork.

name: Kingdom of Scotland
note: Kenneth MacAlpin united the Picts and Gaels. Over centuries the court Normanised and Scots (a Germanic dialect) replaced Gaelic as the lowland tongue, while Gaelic persisted in the Highlands.

name: Yadava Dynasty (Devagiri)
note: Major Deccan dynasty ruling from Devagiri (Daulatabad). Patrons of Marathi literature — the saint-poet Dnyaneshwar wrote under their rule. Conquered by Alauddin Khalji's Delhi Sultanate general Malik Kafur (1307–1317).

name: Saffarid Dynasty
note: Founded by Ya'qub ibn al-Layth al-Saffar, a coppersmith (saffar) turned warlord from Sistan. First dynasty to use New Persian as court language instead of Arabic. Conquered Kabul and ended the Zunbil dynasty. Defeated by the Samanids but survived as vassals in Sistan.

name: Tulunid Dynasty
note: First independent Muslim dynasty in Egypt. Founded by Ahmad ibn Tulun, a Turkic slave-soldier of the Abbasid caliphate. Built the Mosque of Ibn Tulun in Cairo — one of the oldest intact mosques in Egypt. Controlled Egypt and Syria.

name: Kingdom of Norway
note: Harald Fairhair unified Norway c.872. Norway entered personal union with Denmark in 1380 after the Oldenburg succession; effectively under Danish rule until 1814.

name: Kievan Rus
note: Founded by Varangian (Norse) Rurikids who were rapidly absorbed into Slavic culture. Christianisation under Vladimir I in 988 tied Rus to Constantinople and Eastern Orthodoxy — the defining civilisational choice. Fragmented into competing principalities after Yaroslav's death; destroyed by the Mongol invasion 1237–1241.

name: Bagratid Armenia
note: Armenian and Georgian Bagratid dynasties rebuilt Christian statehood after the Arab conquest. The Armenian Bagratids (885-1045) and their Georgian branch created the 'Armenian Renaissance' with Ani as a capital that rivalled Constantinople. Ani fell to the Seljuk Turks in 1064, effectively ending Armenian political independence for centuries.

name: Kingdom of Bohemia
note: Přemyslid dynasty (895–1306), then Luxembourg (1310–1437). Bohemia became the intellectual centre of the HRE under Charles IV. The Hussite movement (1419–1434) was the first successful proto-Protestant revolt in Europe, a century before Luther.

name: Chimú Kingdom
note: Coastal Andean kingdom conquered by Inca

name: Postclassic Maya (Mayapan, K'iche')
note: Postclassic Maya kingdoms

name: Toltec Empire
note: Tula-based predecessor to Aztecs

name: Zagwe Dynasty
note: Ethiopian dynasty ruling from Roha (now Lalibela). King Lalibela commissioned the eleven rock-hewn churches of Lalibela — carved from solid volcanic rock, they are among the most remarkable architectural achievements in Africa. Overthrown by the Solomonic restoration.

name: Liao Dynasty (Khitan)
note: Dual administration: Khitan customs for nomads, Chinese Confucian system for Han subjects; Buddhism served as a bridge between nomadic and sedentary cultures

name: Goryeo Dynasty
note: Founded by Wang Geon; successfully resisted multiple Mongol (Yuan) invasions, eventually becoming a vassal-son-in-law state of the Yuan. Known for the Tripitaka Koreana and Goryeo celadon.

name: Croatian Kingdom
note: Tomislav was the first Croatian king (925). The kingdom reached its peak under Petar Kresimir IV. After a succession crisis, Croatia entered a personal union with Hungary (1102) that lasted until 1918. The Pacta Conventa defined Croatian autonomy within the union.

name: Kingdom of England
note: Founded by Æthelstan; the Norman Conquest of 1066 replaced the Anglo-Saxon ruling class with a French-speaking Norman aristocracy, creating the distinctive Anglo-Norman culture. By 1300 English had re-emerged as the dominant tongue with heavy French vocabulary.

name: Buyid Dynasty
note: Daylamite (Iranian) dynasty that conquered Baghdad and reduced the Abbasid caliph to a figurehead. The first major Shia dynasty. Patronised Persian literature and science — the philosopher Avicenna served Buyid courts. Overthrown by the Seljuk Turks.

name: Ikhshidid Dynasty
note: Short-lived Turkic dynasty ruling Egypt and Syria. The regent Kafur (an Ethiopian eunuch) was the effective ruler and one of the most powerful Black rulers in medieval Islamic history. Overthrown by the Fatimid invasion from North Africa.

name: Kingdom of Denmark
note: The dominant Scandinavian power through the Middle Ages; ruled England 1013–1042 (Cnut); lost Sweden definitively in 1658; retains Greenland and Faroe Islands.

name: Holy Roman Empire
note: Neither holy, Roman, nor an empire — Voltaire. A decentralized confederation of German princes, bishops, and city-states under an elected emperor. German princes systematically resisted centralisation; the Peace of Westphalia (1648) effectively made the HRE a loose alliance of sovereign states.

name: Kingdom of Poland
note: Piast dynasty (966–1370), then Jagiellonian (1386–1569). Mieszko I's baptism in 966 is the founding moment of the Polish state. The Union of Lublin (1569) transformed the Polish-Lithuanian personal union into a real Commonwealth.

name: Kingdom of Sweden
note: Broke from the Kalmar Union in 1523 under Gustav Vasa; became a major European Great Power in the 17th century (Swedish Empire, Stormaktstiden); lost Finland to Russia in 1809.

name: Zirid Dynasty
note: Sanhaja Berber dynasty ruling Ifriqiya as Fatimid vassals, then independently. Their break from the Fatimids provoked the devastating Banu Hilal Arab invasion (1057) — one of the most destructive events in North African history, turning agricultural land to pasture.

name: Western Chalukya Empire
note: Revived Chalukya dynasty (Kalyani Chalukyas). Dominated the Deccan for two centuries. Constant warfare with the Cholas to the south. Their collapse fragmented the Deccan into the Hoysala, Kakatiya, and Yadava kingdoms.

name: Ghaznavid Empire
note: Founded by Sebüktigin, a Turkish slave-soldier (ghulam) who rose to govern Ghazni (modern Afghanistan) under the Samanids. His son Mahmud of Ghazni (998–1030) transformed it into a great empire, launching 17 raids into the Indian subcontinent — breaking the Hindu Shahi kingdoms and establishing Islam in the Punjab. The Ghaznavids were patrons of Persian language and literature (the Shahnameh was written for Mahmud). Destroyed by the Ghurids.

name: Kingdom of France
note: Capetian (987–1328), Valois (1328–1589), Bourbon (1589–1792) — three dynasties but one continuous institution. The most powerful western European monarchy of the High and Late Middle Ages. Absolute monarchy reached its apogee under Louis XIV.

name: Kingdom of Hungary
note: Founded by Stephen I who received a crown from Pope Sylvester II. Medieval Hungary was one of the largest European kingdoms, stretching from the Adriatic to Transylvania. The Battle of Mohács (1526) ended the medieval kingdom; Hungary was split between Ottoman and Habsburg rule.

name: Hoysala Empire
note: South Indian dynasty based at Halebidu. Famous for exquisitely ornate temples at Belur and Halebidu. Defeated by the Delhi Sultanate's Malik Kafur expedition (1311). Remnants absorbed into the Vijayanagara Empire.

name: Seljuk Empire
note: Oghuz Turkic rulers adopted Persian as court language; patronized Persian scholars — Omar Khayyam, al-Ghazali, Nizam al-Mulk all flourished under Seljuk rule

name: Western Xia (Tangut Empire)
note: Tangut empire in northwestern China. Controlled the Hexi Corridor and Silk Road trade. Developed a unique script modelled on Chinese characters. Resisted both the Song and Liao dynasties. Utterly destroyed by Genghis Khan's final campaign (1227) — the Mongols annihilated the Tangut state so thoroughly that it nearly vanished from historical memory.

name: Almoravid Dynasty
note: Saharan Berber dynasty founded on strict Maliki Islamic reform. Yusuf ibn Tashfin defeated Alfonso VI at the Battle of Sagrajas (1086) and halted the Reconquista. Founded Marrakesh as capital. Controlled Morocco, western Algeria, and Muslim Spain (al-Andalus).

name: Sena Dynasty
note: Last major Hindu dynasty of Bengal. Patronised Sanskrit literature and codified Hindu caste law (Dayabhaga system). Lakshmana Sena's court included the great poet Jayadeva (Gita Govinda). Collapsed under Bakhtiyar Khalji's Muslim invasion (1203–1204).

name: Sultanate of Rum
note: Rumi composed his poetry in Persian at this court in Konya

name: Gajapati Dynasty (Odisha)
note: Eastern Indian dynasty ruling from Cuttack. Kapilendra Deva expanded the kingdom to its greatest extent. Patrons of the Jagannath temple at Puri. The title 'Gajapati' (Lord of Elephants) was one of the four traditional Indian royal titles.

name: Kakatiya Dynasty
note: Telugu dynasty ruling from Warangal (Orugallu). Built the Warangal Fort and the Thousand Pillar Temple. Rudrama Devi was one of the few female rulers in Indian history. Conquered by the Delhi Sultanate under Ulugh Khan (1323).

name: Kingdom of Jerusalem
note: Crusader state founded after the First Crusade's capture of Jerusalem (1099). Godfrey of Bouillon was the first ruler. The kingdom was a feudal patchwork dependent on Italian maritime republics. Lost Jerusalem to Saladin (1187); briefly recovered it by treaty (1229–1244). The last stronghold, Acre, fell in 1291.

name: Jin Dynasty (Jurchen)
note: Jurchen rulers resisted Sinicization; maintained Jurchen script and shamanistic traditions alongside Buddhism; used a dual admin system similar to Liao

name: Almohad Caliphate
note: Founded by Ibn Tumart's Masmuda Berber religious movement proclaiming Almohad (monotheist) theology. Defeated the Almoravids. At its peak ruled from Iberia to Libya — the largest Berber empire in history. Defeated at Las Navas de Tolosa (1212) which broke Almohad power in Iberia.

name: Qara Khitai (Western Liao)
note: Empire founded by Khitan refugees from the Jurchen conquest of the Liao dynasty. Yelu Dashi defeated the Seljuk Sultan Sanjar at the Battle of Qatwan (1141) — reverberating through Christendom as the legend of Prester John. Conquered by the Mongols (Kuchlug and then Genghis Khan).

name: Zangid Atabegate
note: Turkic dynasty founded by Imad ad-Din Zengi. His son Nur ad-Din unified Muslim Syria and launched the counter-crusade. Nur ad-Din's general Saladin eventually founded the Ayyubid dynasty. The Zangids initiated the ideological framework for reconquering Crusader states.

name: Novgorod Republic
note: One of the most important medieval Russian polities. Governed by a popular assembly (veche) and elected posadniks. A major trading partner of the Hanseatic League. Survived the Mongol invasion by paying tribute without being sacked. Annexed by Ivan III of Moscow in 1478.

name: Ghorid Sultanate
note: Afghan dynasty from Ghor (central Afghanistan). Muhammad of Ghor defeated the Rajput confederacy at the Second Battle of Tarain (1192) and established Muslim rule over the Ganges plain. His general Qutb ud-Din Aibak founded the Delhi Sultanate. The Ghorids destroyed the last Buddhist monasteries at Nalanda and Vikramashila.

name: Angevin Empire
note: The Plantagenet dynasty empire under Henry II (who married Eleanor of Aquitaine), controlling Normandy, Anjou, Maine, Touraine, Poitou, and Aquitaine across the Channel plus England. A cross-Channel feudal aggregate rather than a unified state — conflict with the French crown over the vassal relationship was constant. John's loss of Normandy and Angevin lands in France (1202–1204) ended the continental empire; England persisted as the Plantagenet realm.

name: Ayyubid Sultanate
note: Saladin was Kurdish but the court operated fully in Arabic-Islamic culture

name: Feudal Japan
note: The Kamakura, Muromachi, and Sengoku periods. Decentralized feudal system with competing daimyo (lords) and regional warlords. Ends with the unification by Oda Nobunaga and Toyotomi Hideyoshi, preparing ground for Tokugawa shogunate.

name: Second Bulgarian Empire
note: Restored Bulgarian state after the Asen brothers' rebellion against Byzantium. Tsar Kaloyan defeated the Latin Emperor Baldwin I at Adrianople (1205). Ivan Asen II (r.1218–1241) dominated the Balkans. Fragmented in the 14th century and conquered by the Ottomans.

name: Serbian Empire (Nemanjić Dynasty)
note: Stefan Nemanja founded the medieval Serbian state (1168). His son Sava established the Serbian Orthodox Church (1219) — the key institution of Serbian national identity. Stefan Dušan (1331–1355) expanded Serbia to its greatest extent (Macedonia, Albania, Greece) and crowned himself Emperor of the Serbs and Greeks. The empire collapsed after his death; the Battle of Kosovo (1389) against the Ottomans ended Serbian independence for five centuries.

name: Principality of Galicia-Volhynia
note: Major western Rus' principality. Daniel of Galicia received a royal crown from the Pope (1253). A culturally sophisticated state that maintained contacts with both Latin Christendom and the Mongol Golden Horde. Partitioned between Poland and Lithuania after the last Rurikid ruler died.

name: Carib Peoples
note: Lesser Antilles and coastal South America

name: Khwarazmian Empire
note: Shah Ala ad-Din Muhammad II rapidly built a vast empire covering Persia and Central Asia — briefly the most powerful state in the Islamic world. His catastrophic decision to execute Genghis Khan's trade envoys (1218) provoked the Mongol invasion. Within three years his empire was destroyed, millions killed, and the cities of Central Asia — Samarkand, Bukhara, Merv, Nishapur — were annihilated. The Khwarazmian destruction was one of the worst demographic catastrophes in history.

name: Taíno
note: Arawak peoples of the Caribbean

name: Latin Empire
note: Crusader state established after the Fourth Crusade's sack of Constantinople (1204). A catastrophic blow to Byzantine civilization. Baldwin of Flanders was the first Latin Emperor. Constantly under pressure from the Byzantine successor states (Nicaea, Epirus, Trebizond). Recaptured by Michael VIII Palaiologos of Nicaea in 1261.

name: Delhi Sultanate
note: Established the Persianate Islamic cultural layer in India; Persian as court language; laid the groundwork for the Hindu-Muslim civilizational divide that defines the subcontinent to this day

name: Mongol Empire
note: Religiously tolerant by policy (Yasa law); the different successor khanates each converted to the local dominant religion

name: Chagatai Khanate
note: Chagatai Turkic became the prestige literary language of Central Asia after Mongol Turkification

name: Hafsid Dynasty
note: Almohad governors who became independent rulers of Ifriqiya (Tunisia and eastern Algeria). At one point recognised as caliph by Mecca. Tunis became a major Mediterranean trading city. Conquered by the Ottoman corsair Hayreddin Barbarossa.

name: Teutonic Order (Monastic State)
note: Crusading military order invited to Christianise the pagan Old Prussians; completed the conquest by 1283 (exterminating or assimilating the indigenous Prussians). At their peak controlled the entire Baltic coast from Danzig to Estonia. Catastrophically defeated at Grunwald (1410) by Poland-Lithuania; dissolved its monastic state and became a secular Duchy of Prussia in 1525.

name: Grand Duchy of Lithuania
note: The last pagan state in Europe, Christianised only in 1387 when Jogaila married Jadwiga of Poland. At its peak the Grand Duchy stretched from the Baltic to the Black Sea, encompassing most of modern Ukraine and Belarus. Its administrative language was Ruthenian (Old East Slavic), not Lithuanian.

name: Golden Horde
note: Converted to Sunni Islam ~1313 under Özbeg Khan; Mongol rulers gradually Turkified — adopted Kipchak Turkic as the dominant language

name: Marinid Dynasty
note: Zenata Berber dynasty that replaced the Almohads in Morocco. Built the famous Bou Inania Madrasa in Fez. Attempted to reconquer al-Andalus but defeated at the Battle of Rio Salado (1340). Their decline opened Morocco to Portuguese coastal incursions.

name: Mamluk Sultanate
note: Turkic slave-soldier rulers but court and administration were Arabic-Islamic

name: Ilkhanate
note: Started Tengriist/Buddhist; converted to Sunni Islam and were fully Persianized — Persian remained the language of court and literature

name: Ethiopian Empire (Solomonic)
note: Claimed descent from King Solomon and the Queen of Sheba. The longest-ruling dynasty in world history. Emperor Menelik II defeated Italy at Adwa (1896) — the most decisive African victory over a European power. Haile Selassie modernised Ethiopia and inspired the Rastafari movement. Ended by the Derg communist revolution.

name: Yuan Dynasty
note: Kublai Khan adopted Tibetan Buddhism (Vajrayana) as state religion and Phags-pa Lama as spiritual authority; deliberately avoided Sinicization — Chinese barred from top posts; Mongol identity maintained as ruling class

name: Ifat Sultanate
note: Medieval Muslim sultanate on the Ethiopian coast. Fought prolonged wars with the Solomonic Ethiopian Empire. The Walashma dynasty ruled both Ifat and its successor state Adal. A crucial link in Indian Ocean trade networks.

name: Principality of Wallachia
note: Romanian principality between the Carpathians and the Danube. Mircea the Elder (r.1386–1418) and Vlad III ('the Impaler' / Dracula, r.1456–1462) resisted Ottoman expansion. Wallachia was an Ottoman vassal for most of its history. United with Moldavia to form Romania (1859).

name: Ottoman Empire
note: Claimed the Caliphate title after conquering Mamluk Egypt (1517). Unlike earlier Turkic states, developed a distinct Turkic-Islamic identity rather than Persianizing. Ruled Orthodox Christians, Catholics, Jews, and Arabs as separate millets (religious communities) — religion defined the subject's legal identity, not ethnicity

name: Ajuran Sultanate
note: Somali sultanate controlling the Indian Ocean coast. A hydraulic empire that built sophisticated water engineering systems. Controlled Mogadishu, then one of the wealthiest cities in Africa. Traded extensively with Ming China, Mughal India, and Mamluk Egypt.

name: Jalayirid Sultanate
note: Mongol successor state in Iraq and western Iran after the Il-Khanate's collapse. Ahmad Jalayir patronised Persian miniature painting — the Baghdad school flourished under his rule. Destroyed by Timur's invasions and Turkmen tribal pressure.

name: Madurai Sultanate
note: Short-lived sultanate established after the Delhi Sultanate's Tughluq governors declared independence. Destroyed by the rising Vijayanagara Empire under Kumara Kampana — celebrated in the Tamil epic Madhuravijayam.

name: Vijayanagara Empire
note: The last great Hindu empire; for three centuries it was the bulwark of Hindu civilisation against the Muslim Deccan sultanates. Founded by brothers Harihara and Bukka after escaping Delhi Sultanate captivity. The Battle of Talikota (1565) destroyed the capital Hampi — the ruins remain one of India's great historical sites.

name: Principality of Moldavia
note: Romanian principality east of the Carpathians. Stephen the Great (r.1457–1504) defeated the Ottomans, Hungarians, and Poles — Pope Sixtus IV called him 'Athlete of Christ'. Moldavia was an Ottoman vassal from 1538. United with Wallachia to form Romania (1859).

name: Bahmani Sultanate
note: The first major Muslim sultanate of the Deccan; founded by a revolt against Delhi. Fragmented into five successor sultanates (Adil Shahi, Nizam Shahi, Qutb Shahi, Imad Shahi, Barid Shahi) which fought Vijayanagara and each other for two centuries. Bidar was its capital.

name: Moghulistan Khanate
note: Eastern successor of the Chagatai Khanate. The Moghul khans (Chagataid Mongols who had Islamised and Turkicised) controlled Xinjiang and parts of Central Asia. Tughlugh Timur was the first to convert to Islam. Fragmented into smaller khanates.

name: Bengal Sultanate
note: Independent Muslim state in Bengal. Initially Ilyas Shahi dynasty, later the Hussain Shahi dynasty patronised Bengali literature and architecture. Sonargaon and Gaur were successive capitals. Conquered by the Mughals under Akbar.

name: Ming Dynasty
note: Founded as an explicit rejection of Mongol rule; strong Han nativist and Confucian identity; the 'most Chinese' of dynasties

name: North Yuan
note: After the Ming drove the Yuan from China (1368), the Mongol khans retreated to the steppe and continued to claim the title of Great Khan of the Mongol Empire. Periodic reunifications under strong khans (Dayan Khan, Altan Khan) challenged Ming China. Tibetan Buddhism progressively replaced shamanism/Tengriism. Formally ended when the Qing incorporated most Mongol tribes (1635-1691).

name: Timurid Empire
note: Timur was Barlas Turkic; court was thoroughly Persian — Samarkand and Herat became the greatest centers of Persian poetry and miniature painting

name: Kara Koyunlu (Black Sheep Turkmen)
note: Turkmen tribal confederation in eastern Anatolia, Azerbaijan, and Iraq. Jahan Shah briefly controlled a vast territory from Baghdad to Kerman. Patrons of Turkmen and Persian art. Destroyed by their rivals the Aq Qoyunlu at the Battle of Chapakchur (1467).

name: Bosnian Kingdom
note: Tvrtko I crowned himself King of Bosnia and Serbia (1377). The Bosnian Church (a disputed Christian sect) was unique to the region. Fell to the Ottoman Sultan Mehmed II (1463). Many Bosnian nobles converted to Islam — the origin of the Bosniak Muslim community.

name: Aq Qoyunlu (White Sheep Turkmen)
note: Turkmen tribal confederation that dominated Iran and Iraq. Uzun Hasan allied with Venice against the Ottomans. Defeated the Kara Koyunlu and Timurids. Their collapse enabled the Safavid rise — Shah Ismail I defeated the last Aq Qoyunlu ruler at the Battle of Sharur (1501).

name: Joseon Dynasty
note: Founded by Yi Seong-gye; shifted from Buddhist to Neo-Confucian ideology. Known for its stable bureaucracy and the invention of Hangul.

name: Malwa Sultanate
note: Independent sultanate in central India. Hoshang Shah built the Jama Masjid of Mandu — one of the finest examples of Afghan architecture in India. The kingdom was a centre of Indo-Islamic cultural synthesis. Conquered by the Mughals.

name: Jaunpur Sultanate
note: Known as the 'Shiraz of India' for its cultural achievements. The Sharqi dynasty patronised architecture (Atala Mosque) and Islamic scholarship. Conquered by the Lodi Sultans of Delhi.

name: Kalmar Union
note: Personal union of Denmark, Norway, and Sweden under one crown, dominated by Denmark. Sweden repeatedly rebelled; the Stockholm Bloodbath of 1520 (Danish massacre of Swedish nobles) triggered the final Swedish revolt under Gustav Vasa.

name: Mysore Kingdom
note: South Indian kingdom in modern Karnataka. The Wodeyar dynasty ruled until Hyder Ali's usurpation (1761). Hyder Ali and his son Tipu Sultan modernised the army with iron-cased rockets and French-trained infantry. Tipu Sultan died at the Fourth Anglo-Mysore War (1799); the Wodeyars were restored as British vassals.

name: Mapuche Nation
note: Southern Chile and Argentina

name: Serbian Despotate
note: Last medieval Serbian state, ruled by the Lazarevic and Brankovic dynasties after the Ottoman defeat at Ankara (1402) gave a reprieve. Stefan Lazarevic made Belgrade the capital. Gradually reduced by Ottoman expansion until the fall of Smederevo (1459).

name: Gujarat Sultanate
note: Wealthy sultanate controlling Gujarat's lucrative Indian Ocean trade. Ahmad Shah I founded Ahmedabad (1411). Mahmud Begada fortified the coast against Portuguese encroachment. Annexed by the Mughal emperor Akbar.

name: Adal Sultanate
note: Muslim sultanate in the Horn of Africa. Imam Ahmad ibn Ibrahim al-Ghazi ('Ahmad Gran') conquered most of Ethiopia in a devastating jihad (1529–1543), nearly destroying the Solomonic dynasty. Portuguese intervention and Ethiopian resistance eventually reversed the conquest.

name: Aztec Empire (Mexico-Tenochtitlan)
note: Triple Alliance

name: Ryukyu Kingdom
note: Unified the three kingdoms of Okinawa (Sanzan period) under Sho Hashi. A major maritime trading state linking Japan, China, Korea, and Southeast Asia. Became a tributary of Ming China while maintaining independence. Invaded by Japan's Satsuma domain in 1609 and became a dual tributary of both China and Japan. Formally annexed by Japan as Okinawa Prefecture in 1879.

name: Habsburg Monarchy
note: The House of Habsburg held the HRE throne almost continuously 1438–1806. After Napoleon dissolved the HRE, the Habsburgs created the Austrian Empire (1804), then the Austro-Hungarian Dual Monarchy (1867–1918). 'Others wage war; you, happy Austria, marry' — AEIOU policy of dynastic expansion through marriage.

name: Inca Empire (Tawantinsuyu)
note: Largest pre-Columbian empire

name: Kazan Khanate
note: Tatar successor state of the Golden Horde on the middle Volga. A wealthy trading state controlling the Volga-Kama confluence. Ivan the Terrible's conquest of Kazan (1552) was a pivotal moment in Russian expansion eastward. The siege is commemorated by St. Basil's Cathedral in Moscow.

name: Crimean Khanate
note: Last surviving Mongol successor state on the Pontic steppe; Ottoman vassal from 1475. Conducted slave raids into Ukraine, Poland, and Russia — the Crimean slave trade depopulated vast areas of Eastern Europe. Peter the Great and Catherine the Great progressively curtailed Crimean power; annexed by Russia in 1783.

name: Iroquois Confederacy (Haudenosaunee)
note: Five/Six Nations confederacy

name: Kazakh Khanate
note: Emerged from the Uzbek (Shaybanid) breakup when Janybek and Giray led a nomadic confederation into the Kazakh steppe. The three zhuz (hordes: Great, Middle, Small) structure defined Kazakh society. Gradually subordinated by Russian expansion; the Small Zhuz accepted Russian protectorate in 1731 under Dzungar pressure.

name: Spanish Caribbean (Cuba/Hispaniola/Puerto Rico)
note: Spanish colonial islands

name: Khanate of Bukhara
note: Uzbek Shaybanid state centred on Bukhara. Muhammad Shaybani defeated the Timurids and established Uzbek rule over Transoxiana. Bukhara remained a major centre of Islamic learning. The later Janid dynasty was replaced by the Manghit Emirs (Emirate of Bukhara, 1785–1920).

name: Portuguese Brazil
note: Portuguese colonial territory

name: Safavid Empire
note: Established Twelver Shia as state religion; this religious identity is what NOW defines Iran as distinct from Sunni Turkic/Arab neighbors — same Persian ethnicity as the Timurids but a completely different civilizational identity

name: Funj Sultanate
note: Major sultanate of the Nile Valley (modern Sudan). Founded by the Funj people at Sennar. An alliance with the Abdallabi Arabs created a vast state. The sultanate Islamised much of the upper Nile region. Conquered by Muhammad Ali's Egyptian forces.

name: Khanate of Khiva
note: Uzbek khanate in Khwarezm (modern western Uzbekistan/Turkmenistan). Controlled the lower Amu Darya. The slave trade was a major economic activity. Russia's General Kaufman conquered Khiva in 1873, making it a protectorate. Abolished by the Red Army (1920).

name: Spanish Florida
note: Spanish colonial territory

name: Yarkand Khanate
note: Chagataid khanate in the Tarim Basin (southern Xinjiang). Said Khan founded the khanate after defeating the Moghulistan remnants. The Naqshbandi Sufi order gained enormous influence. Conquered by the Dzungar Khanate.

name: Viceroyalty of New Spain
note: Spanish colonial empire in the Americas

name: Mughal Empire
note: Babur was Timurid (Turkic-Mongol); court was thoroughly Persianized. Akbar's syncretic 'Din-i-Ilahi' experiment attempted to bridge Hindu and Muslim identities — shows religion as a live political variable even within a single regime

name: New France
note: French colonial territory

name: Cherokee Nation
note: Pre-removal Cherokee territory

name: Sur Empire
note: Afghan dynasty founded by Sher Shah Suri after defeating the Mughal Humayun. Despite ruling only 16 years, Sher Shah introduced the rupee, built the Grand Trunk Road, and reformed the revenue system. These innovations were adopted by the returning Mughals and later the British.

name: Viceroyalty of Peru
note: Spanish colonial South America

name: Saadian Dynasty
note: Arab-descended Sharifs who expelled the Portuguese from Morocco. Ahmad al-Mansur's conquest of the Songhai Empire (1591) brought trans-Saharan gold wealth. Built the magnificent El Badi Palace in Marrakesh. The dynasty's collapse led to a prolonged civil war.

name: Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth
note: The Rzeczpospolita — an elected monarchy (Sejm elected the king) with the liberum veto, which allowed any single noble to dissolve the Sejm. The world's first country with a modern written constitution (May 3, 1791). Partitioned three times by Russia, Prussia, and Austria (1772, 1793, 1795) — erased from the map for 123 years.

name: Creek Confederacy (Muscogee)
note: Southeastern confederacy

name: Darfur Sultanate
note: Fur dynasty controlling western Sudan. The Keira dynasty built a powerful state based on trans-Saharan trade. Ahmad Bukr introduced Islam. Conquered by the British-Egyptian Mahdist War aftermath but briefly revived (1898–1916). Its legacy shapes Darfur's political identity today.

name: Tokugawa Shogunate (Edo Period)
note: A period of isolation (Sakoku) and peace under the Tokugawa shoguns. Decentralized but tightly controlled feudalism.

name: Jamestown Colony
note: First permanent English settlement

name: Thirteen Colonies
note: British colonies on Atlantic seaboard

name: Plymouth Colony
note: Pilgrim settlement

name: British Caribbean
note: British colonial islands

name: New Amsterdam (Dutch)
note: Dutch colonial New York

name: Alawite Dynasty (Morocco)
note: Ruling dynasty of Morocco since the 17th century — one of the world's longest-reigning dynasties. Moulay Ismail (r.1672–1727) built a formidable army and made Meknes a rival to Versailles. The current King Mohammed VI traces direct descent. Morocco is one of only three kingdoms remaining in Africa.

name: Dzungar Khanate
note: Last great nomadic empire of Central Asia. The Oirat Mongol Dzungars controlled a vast territory from Kazakhstan to Tibet. Their wars with the Qing dynasty spanned a century. The Qing genocide of the Dzungars (1755–1758) killed an estimated 80% of the Dzungar population — one of the 18th century's greatest atrocities.

name: French Caribbean
note: Martinique/Guadeloupe

name: Khoshut Khanate
note: Oirat Mongol khanate in Tibet. Gushi Khan installed the 5th Dalai Lama as ruler of Tibet (1642), creating the Ganden Phodrang government. The Khoshuts served as military protectors of the Dalai Lama's regime until overthrown by Dzungar invaders (1717).

name: Qing Dynasty (Manchu)
note: Manchu rulers governed Han majority but maintained strict Manchu identity: forbidden intermarriage, maintained bannerman system, Manchu language in court. Vajrayana Buddhism was a deliberate tool to legitimize rule over Mongols and Tibetans. Projected Confucian face outward while preserving Manchu identity internally — a masterclass in dual identity governance

name: Cossack Hetmanate
note: Ukrainian Cossack state founded by Bohdan Khmelnytsky's uprising against Poland (1648). The Treaty of Pereyaslav (1654) brought Russian protection — and eventual domination. The Hetmanate represented the first major expression of Ukrainian political autonomy. Abolished by Catherine the Great.

name: Rupert's Land (Hudson's Bay Company)
note: HBC fur trade territory

name: Maratha Empire
note: The Marathas challenged Mughal dominance and nearly unified India under Hindu rule before the British arrived. At their peak (1760) they controlled most of the subcontinent. The Third Battle of Panipat (1761) against the Afghans shattered Maratha power and opened the door for British expansion.

name: Sioux Nations (Lakota/Dakota)
note: Plains nations

name: Louisiana Territory (French)
note: French then briefly Spanish territory

name: Kingdom of Great Britain
note: The Acts of Union 1707 merged England and Scotland into a single kingdom with one parliament. Scotland retained its distinct legal and church systems.

name: Hotak Dynasty
note: Ghilzai Pashtun dynasty that overthrew Safavid rule. Mirwais Hotak revolted in Kandahar (1709). His son Mahmud conquered Isfahan (1722) and ended the Safavid dynasty — one of the most dramatic reversals in Persian history. Defeated by Nadir Shah (Afsharid) by 1738.

name: Khanate of Kokand
note: Uzbek khanate in the Fergana Valley. Expanded into Tashkent and parts of southern Kazakhstan and Xinjiang. The most commercially active of the three Central Asian khanates. Conquered by the Russian Empire under General Skobelev (1876).

name: Viceroyalty of New Granada
note: Spanish colonial northern South America

name: Russian Empire
note: Peter the Great declared the Russian Empire in 1721 after the Great Northern War victory over Sweden. Peter's Westernisation programme transformed Russia into a European Great Power. Catherine the Great (1762–1796) expanded the empire south (Crimea) and west (Poland partitions). 19th-century Russia was the largest state in the world by land area. Defeated in Crimea (1853–56) and Japan (1904–5); autocracy resisted reform until too late; collapsed in Revolution 1917.

name: Nizam of Hyderabad
note: Founded by Asaf Jah I (Nizam-ul-Mulk) after the Mughal Empire's decline. The richest princely state in British India. The Nizam was reputedly the wealthiest man in the world in the 1930s. Integrated into India by military action (Operation Polo) in 1948.

name: Russian America
note: Russian colonial Alaska

name: Durrani Empire
note: Founded by Ahmad Shah Durrani after the assassination of Nadir Shah. Ahmad Shah united the Pashtun tribes and created the first unified Afghan state. His empire stretched from eastern Iran to northern India and from the Amu Darya to the Indian Ocean. The Koh-i-Noor diamond was taken from the Mughal court. The empire fragmented after his death into competing Durrani factions.

name: Zand Dynasty
note: Short-lived Persian dynasty. Karim Khan Zand ruled as 'Advocate of the People' (Vakil) rather than shah — a unique humility. Made Shiraz a cultural capital. The Vakil Bazaar and citadel remain landmarks. Overthrown by the Qajar Agha Muhammad Khan in a brutal campaign.

name: Viceroyalty of Río de la Plata
note: Spanish colonial southern South America

name: Kingdom of Hawaiʻi
note: Unified Hawaiian islands under Kamehameha

name: Sikh Empire
note: Ranjit Singh unified the Sikh misls (confederacies) into the last major pre-British Indian empire. The Sikh army (Khalsa) trained by European officers was the most modern in Asia. The two Anglo-Sikh Wars ended Sikh independence — the Koh-i-Noor diamond was ceded to the British Crown.

name: United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland
note: Acts of Union 1800 added Ireland; the Irish Free State left in 1922, creating the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland.

name: Republic of Haiti
note: First Black republic

name: Kingdom of Egypt
note: Muhammad Ali Pasha (an Albanian Ottoman officer) seized power in 1805 and founded a modernising dynasty that ruled Egypt for 147 years. He built a modern army, industrialised cotton, and made Egypt semi-independent of Constantinople. His descendants built the Suez Canal (opened 1869) but went bankrupt — British occupation followed (1882). Egypt became a Kingdom nominally independent in 1922 but with British troops remaining. Farouk's corruption and the 1948 Arab–Israeli War defeat triggered the 1952 military coup.

name: Gran Colombia
note: Bolívar's united republic

name: First Mexican Empire
note: Iturbide's short-lived empire

name: Empire of Brazil
note: Independent Brazilian monarchy

name: Kingdom of Greece
note: Established after the Greek War of Independence (1821–1829). The Megali Idea drove territorial expansion through the Balkan Wars and WWI. Oscillated between monarchy and republic. The military junta (1967–1974) was the final crisis; a referendum abolished the monarchy in 1974.

name: Republic of Texas
note: Independent republic before US annexation

name: California Republic
note: Brief independence before US annexation

name: British Raj
note: The British Crown replaced the East India Company after the Sepoy Mutiny (1857). The Raj unified the subcontinent under a single administration for the first time since Ashoka — railways, telegraph, common law, English education — but extraction (the 'drain of wealth' Dadabhai Naoroji documented) kept India poor. The Bengal famine of 1943 killed 2–3 million under Churchill's watch. Independence movement from the Indian National Congress; Gandhi's non-violent resistance made the British position untenable.

name: Confederate States of America
note: Secessionist state during Civil War

name: Kingdom of Italy
note: The Risorgimento unified the Italian peninsula for the first time since the Western Roman Empire. Italy joined WWI on the Allied side but felt cheated of territory at Versailles ('mutilated victory'), fuelling the rise of Fascism. Mussolini's regime allied with Nazi Germany; ended with Allied liberation and a 1946 referendum establishing the Republic.

name: Second Mexican Empire
note: Maximilian's French-backed empire

name: Dominion of Canada
note: British dominion

name: Empire of Japan (Meiji)
note: Rapid modernization (Meiji Restoration) and transformation into a world power. Dismantled the feudal system and expanded across East Asia.

name: German Empire (Deutsches Reich)
note: Proclaimed in the Hall of Mirrors at Versailles after the Franco-Prussian War — deliberately humiliating for France. A Prussian-dominated constitutional monarchy; industrialised at breakneck speed to become Europe's foremost industrial power. Bismarck's system of alliances held Europe in balance until Wilhelm II dismissed him in 1890.

name: Kingdom of Romania
note: Created from the union of Wallachia and Moldavia. Gained independence from the Ottoman Empire in 1877. Doubled in size after World War I by acquiring Transylvania, Bessarabia, and Bukovina. Ion Antonescu's alliance with Nazi Germany led to participation in the Holocaust. Communist takeover forced King Michael's abdication (1947).

name: Mahdist State
note: Founded by Muhammad Ahmad (the Mahdi) who proclaimed a religious revolution against Egyptian-Ottoman rule. The siege and fall of Khartoum (1885), killing General Gordon, shocked the British Empire. Destroyed by Kitchener's Anglo-Egyptian force at Omdurman (1898).

name: Republic of Hawaiʻi
note: Post-monarchy republic before US annexation

name: Japanese-Occupied Korea
note: Japanese colonial period following the Japan-Korea Treaty of 1910. Direct imperial administration, suppression of Korean language and culture, and economic exploitation. Ended with Japan's surrender in World War II.

name: Republic of China
note: Succeeded the Qing Dynasty after the 1911 Revolution. Sun Yat-sen's Three Principles (Nationalism, Democracy, People's Livelihood). The ROC ruled mainland China through the warlord era, Northern Expedition (1928), Nanjing Decade of limited modernisation, Japanese War (1937–1945), and Civil War. In 1949 the Communists drove the Nationalist government to Taiwan, where the ROC continues to govern.

name: Czechoslovakia
note: Created from the Habsburg collapse at Versailles — the most successful democratic state in interwar Central Europe. Munich Agreement (1938) forced it to cede the Sudetenland to Hitler; fully occupied 1939. Liberated 1945; Communist coup 1948. The Prague Spring (1968) — Alexander Dubček's 'socialism with a human face' — was crushed by Soviet tanks. Velvet Revolution (1989) ended communism peacefully. Velvet Divorce (1993) split into Czech Republic and Slovakia.

name: Weimar Republic
note: Germany's first democracy, proclaimed on November 9, 1918 as the Kaiser abdicated. Named after Weimar where the constitution was drafted. The republic faced hyperinflation (1923), the Great Depression (1929), political violence from left and right, and structural constitutional weaknesses (Article 48 emergency powers). Despite cultural brilliance (Bauhaus, Expressionism, cabaret), it was too fragile to survive the economic catastrophe. Hitler was appointed Chancellor on January 30, 1933.

name: Kingdom of Iraq
note: Created by Britain from three Ottoman vilayets (Mosul, Baghdad, Basra) under League of Nations Mandate. Faisal I (son of Sharif Hussein) installed as king. Iraq gained nominal independence in 1932 (first Arab state to join the League of Nations) while British advisors and air bases remained. The 1948 Arab–Israeli War and 1958 military coup ended the Hashemite monarchy.

name: Republic of Turkey
note: Mustafa Kemal Atatürk proclaimed the Republic on October 29, 1923 after the War of Independence expelled Greek, French, and Italian forces. Atatürk's programme of Westernisation and secularism: abolished the Caliphate (1924), replaced Arabic script with Latin alphabet, banned the fez, established women's suffrage (1934). Turkey joined NATO (1952). Military coups in 1960, 1971, 1980 periodically interrupted democracy. Erdoğan's AKP government (2002–present) re-Islamised Turkish public life and consolidated presidential power.

name: Mongolian People's Republic
note: Established as a Soviet satellite state after Soviet-backed revolution against the theocratic Bogd Khanate. The purges of the 1930s under Choibalsan (the 'Mongolian Stalin') massacred 30,000–35,000 people, mostly Buddhist monks — 700 monasteries destroyed. Mongolia was a loyal Soviet ally and the second-oldest communist state. Transitioned to democracy in 1990 and formally dissolved in 1992.

name: Manchukuo
note: Japanese puppet state in Manchuria and Inner Mongolia, with the last Qing emperor Puyi as nominal head of state. The Kwantung Army controlled all real power. Site of Unit 731 (biological warfare experiments on prisoners). Collapsed immediately when the Soviet Union declared war on Japan (August 1945) and invaded Manchuria.

name: Kingdom of Saudi Arabia
note: Ibn Saud united the Arabian Peninsula through conquest and treaty (1902–1932). Oil discovered in commercial quantities (1938) transformed the kingdom. The 1973 Oil Embargo made Saudi Arabia a global power. The Grand Mosque seizure (1979) by Juhayman al-Otaibi shocked the kingdom; the regime's response was to give more power to conservative ulema — the origin of Saudi export of Wahhabism worldwide. The kingdom remains an absolute monarchy under the Al Saud family, with Vision 2030 attempting modernisation under Crown Prince MBS.

name: Hashemite Kingdom of Jordan
note: Founded as Transjordan under the Hashemite Emir Abdullah I (British mandate from 1921). Gained independence in 1946. Lost the West Bank to Israel in 1967. Absorbed large Palestinian refugee populations. The Hashemite dynasty traces descent from the Prophet Muhammad.

name: Modern Japan (Postwar)
note: Post-1947 Japan following American occupation and the 1947 Constitution. Parliamentary democracy with a figurehead emperor; economic miracle and rise as a technology and manufacturing superpower.

name: Pakistan
note: Created as a Muslim homeland by the partition of British India. Jinnah's death (1948) left Pakistan without a founder statesman. Three wars with India (1947, 1965, 1971); Bangladesh broke away in 1971 after the Pakistani Army's genocide in East Pakistan. Nuclear power since 1998. Chronic alternation between civilian and military government; the Army as the dominant political institution. ISI intelligence services and Islamist politics complicate democratisation.

name: Republic of India
note: Independent India is the world's largest democracy. Partition from Pakistan (1947) was accompanied by mass violence killing 200,000–2 million and displacing 10–20 million people. Nehru's non-aligned foreign policy, democratic constitution, and planned economy shaped the first decades. Economic liberalisation (1991) transformed India into one of the world's fastest-growing economies. BJP (Hindu nationalist) politics under Modi since 2014.

name: Democratic People's Republic of Korea (North Korea)
State of Israel
note: Founded on May 14, 1948 following the UN Partition Plan and the end of the British Mandate. Wars with Arab neighbours (1948, 1967, 1973) expanded its territory. The Israeli-Palestinian conflict remains one of the world's most intractable disputes. A technologically advanced democracy with compulsory military service.

name: People's Republic of China
note: Mao Zedong proclaimed the PRC on October 1, 1949. The Great Leap Forward (1958–62) caused 15–55 million deaths by famine. The Cultural Revolution (1966–76) destroyed traditional culture and persecuted intellectuals. Deng Xiaoping's economic reforms (1978) launched 40 years of 10% annual growth — the largest poverty reduction in history. Xi Jinping's consolidation of personal power since 2012.

name: Republic of Egypt
note: The Free Officers' Revolution (July 23, 1952) ended the monarchy. Nasser nationalised the Suez Canal (1956), triggering the Suez Crisis — and emerged as pan-Arab hero when US pressure forced Britain and France to withdraw. Nasser's Egypt was the champion of Arab nationalism. Sadat's Camp David Accords (1978) made peace with Israel — and got him assassinated (1981). Mubarak's 30-year autocracy ended with the Arab Spring (2011); democratic opening under Morsi crushed by military coup (2013) under Sisi.

name: Republic of Iraq
note: General Abd al-Karim Qasim's 1958 coup killed the royal family and created the republic. Ba'ath Party coups (1963, 1968) brought Saddam Hussein to power (formally 1979). Iran-Iraq War (1980–88); Kuwait invasion (1990); Gulf War; UN sanctions devastated the country. US invasion (2003) destroyed the state; Sunni insurgency, al-Qaeda, and ISIS emerged from the chaos. The 2005 constitution and Shia-dominated democracy have been unable to overcome sectarian divisions.

name: Canada
note: Full sovereignty with Constitution Act 1982

name: Song Dynasty
note: Founded by Zhao Kuangyin (Emperor Taizu); northern (960–1127) and southern (1127–1279) periods split by Jurchen Jin conquest of the north

name: Bavandid Dynasty
note: Caspian dynasty ruling Tabaristan/Mazandaran; outlasted the Sassanids under Umayyad/Abbasid suzerainty

name: Dabuyid Dynasty
note: Sassanid successor dynasty in Tabaristan; resisted Arab conquest until 760 CE

name: Dulafid Dynasty
note: Arab emirate in Jibal (western Iran) under Abbasid suzerainty

name: Eldiguzid Dynasty (Ildeguzids)
note: Atabeg dynasty of Azerbaijan and Arran; Seljuk successor state

name: Injuid Dynasty
note: Ilkhanate successor dynasty ruling Fars from Shiraz

name: Kakuyid Dynasty
note: Shiite Daylamite dynasty ruling central Iran; reduced to vassals under Seljuks

name: Kar-Kiya Dynasty
note: Zaydi Shiite dynasty of Biyeh-pish (eastern Gilan)

name: Kartid Dynasty
note: Ilkhanate vassal dynasty ruling Khorasan from Herat; ended by Timur

name: Khalji Dynasty
note: Turko-Afghan dynasty of Delhi Sultanate; briefly extended into eastern Iran/Baluchistan

name: Rawwadid Dynasty
note: Kurdicized Arab dynasty of Iranian Azerbaijan; ended by Seljuk conquest

name: Salgurid Dynasty
note: Atabeg dynasty of Fars (Persis); Seljuk/Ilkhanate vassals

name: Zengid Dynasty
note: Oghuz Turk atabeg dynasty of Mosul/Aleppo; absorbed by Ayyubids

name: Ziyarid Dynasty
note: Daylamite dynasty ruling Tabaristan/Gorgan; cultural Persian renaissance patrons

name: Anshan Kingdom
note: Elamite highland kingdom; one of the two capitals of Elam alongside Susa

name: Kassite Babylon
note: Kassite dynasty ruling Babylon; Middle Babylonian period

name: Lullubi Kingdom
note: Highland kingdom in the Zagros mountains; attested in Akkadian and Kassite sources

name: Mannai Kingdom
note: Iron Age kingdom in the Lake Urmia basin; absorbed by Medes

name: Marhashi Kingdom
note: Bronze Age kingdom in southeastern Iran; rival of Akkad and Elam

name: Proto-Elamite
note: Proto-urban civilization of southwestern Iran; earliest writing system in Iran

name: Kidarite Kingdom
note: Hunnic kingdom in Bactria and Gandhara; precursor to the Hephthalites

name: Azerbaijan Peoples Government
note: Soviet-backed separatist state in Iranian Azerbaijan; collapsed after Soviet withdrawal

name: Mahabad Republic
note: Soviet-backed Kurdish republic; collapsed same year as Azerbaijan Peoples Government

name: Kalat Khanate
note: Baloch khanate centered on Kalat; partial sovereignty under Persian/British suzerainty

name: Hormuz Kingdom
note: Persian Gulf kingdom controlling Strait of Hormuz; ended by Safavid-English expulsion of Portuguese

name: Xia Dynasty (夏)
note: Semi-legendary first Chinese dynasty; attested in Zhou-era texts; possible archaeological correlate in Erlitou culture

name: Western Zhou (西周)
note: Founded after the Zhou conquest of Shang; classic feudal system (fengjian); capital at Haojing

name: Eastern Zhou (东周)
note: Continuation of Zhou after move to Luoyang; encompasses Spring and Autumn and Warring States periods

name: Ba and Shu (巴·蜀)
note: Two southwestern states in modern Sichuan; culturally distinct from Zhou heartland; conquered by Qin in 316 BCE

name: Xin Dynasty (新)
note: Wang Mang's usurpation between Western and Eastern Han; radical land and currency reforms; collapsed in red Eyebrows rebellion

name: Xuan Han (玄汉)
note: Gengshi Emperor's short-lived Han restoration between Xin and Eastern Han

name: Eastern Han (东汉)
note: Later Han; restored by Emperor Guangwu; capital moved to Luoyang; ended with warlord fragmentation into Three Kingdoms

name: Shun Dynasty (顺)
note: Li Zicheng's rebel dynasty that captured Beijing and ended the Ming; crushed by Qing-Wu Sangui alliance within the year

name: Former Qin (前秦)
note: Di-ruled state that briefly reunified northern China under Fu Jian; shattered at the Battle of Fei River (383)

name: Liu Song (刘宋)
note: First of the Southern Dynasties; founded by Liu Yu after Eastern Jin; cultural flourishing

name: Southern Qi (南齐)
note: Short-lived second Southern Dynasty; overthrown by Liang's founding emperor

name: Liang Dynasty (梁)
note: Third Southern Dynasty; reign of Emperor Wu of Liang marked Buddhist patronage; ended by Hou Jing rebellion

name: Chen Dynasty (陈)
note: Last of the Southern Dynasties; conquered by Sui in 589 ending 300 years of division

name: Wu Zhou (武周)
note: Empress Wu Zetian's interregnum renaming Tang as Zhou; only female emperor in Chinese history

name: Huan Chu (桓楚)
note: Huan Xuan's brief usurpation of Eastern Jin; reverted to Jin within a year

name: Later Tang (后唐)
note: Shatuo Turk-founded Five Dynasties state claiming Tang legitimacy; controlled north China

name: Later Zhou (后周)
note: Last of the Five Dynasties; reforms under Emperor Shizong paved way for Song unification

name: Southern Ming (南明)
note: Ming loyalist regimes resisting Qing conquest from southern China; final defeat at Battle of Magong

name: Kingdom of Tungning (东宁)
note: Koxinga (Zheng Chenggong) dynasty on Taiwan; last Ming loyalist holdout; annexed by Qing

name: Taiping Heavenly Kingdom (太平天国)
note: Millenarian Christian-inspired rebellion led by Hong Xiuquan; estimated 20-30 million deaths in its suppression

name: Empire of China (Yuan Shikai) (中华帝国)
note: Yuan Shikai's 83-day monarchy restoration; collapsed under military and provincial opposition

name: Beiyang Government (北洋政府)
note: Internationally recognized government of Republic of China dominated by Beiyang warlords; ended by Northern Expedition

name: Nationalist Government (国民政府)
note: Kuomintang-led ROC government; nominally unified China; relocated to Taiwan after Chinese Civil War

name: Wuhan Government (武汉政府)
note: Left-wing KMT government led by Wang Jingwei; rival to Nanjing during KMT-CCP split; merged with Nanjing late 1927

name: Wang Jingwei Regime (汪精卫政权)
note: Japanese puppet Reorganized National Government in Nanjing during Second Sino-Japanese War

name: Chinese Soviet Republic (中华苏维埃)
note: CCP-led proto-state in Jiangxi; Long March followed its defeat by KMT encirclement campaigns

name: Magna Graecia
note: Greek colonial city-states in southern Italy and Sicily (Syracuse

name: Carthaginian Sardinia
note: Punic province on Sardinia prior to Roman annexation

name: Carthaginian Western Sicily
note: Punic-controlled western Sicily (Motya

name: Phoenician Colonies of Sardinia
note: Phoenician trading settlements (Tharros

name: Punic Coastal Settlements
note: Punic coastal trading posts in Italic peninsula

name: Cisalpine Celtic Gauls
note: Insubres / Boii / Senones tribes in Po Valley before Roman subjugation

name: Oscan-Samnite Peoples
note: Central-southern Italic tribal confederations (Samnites

name: Latin-Sabine-Samnite Peoples
note: Pre-Roman Italic peoples of central peninsula

name: Italic Peoples
note: Oscan / Samnite / Lucanian Italic tribal groups

name: Ligurian-Euganei-Raeti Peoples
note: Pre-Roman Alpine and Ligurian peoples of northern Italy

name: Celtic La Tène Migration
note: Celtic La Tène culture expansion into northern Italy

name: Papal States
note: Temporal papal dominion (Donation of Pepin to Italian unification); includes Avignon exile 1309-1377

name: Byzantine Exarchate of Ravenna
note: Byzantine administrative unit centered on Ravenna until Lombard conquest

name: Byzantine Catepanate of Italy
note: Byzantine province in Apulia and Calabria until Norman conquest

name: Byzantine Sardinia
note: Byzantine province on Sardinia after Justinian's reconquest

name: Byzantine Sicilian Theme
note: Byzantine military theme of Sicily until Aghlabid invasion

name: Lombard Duchy of Benevento
note: Lombard duchy in southern Italy surviving the fall of the Lombard Kingdom

name: Lombard Duchy of Spoleto
note: Lombard duchy in central Italy annexed into Carolingian realm

name: Lombard Principalities of Southern Italy
note: Benevento / Salerno / Capua principalities after fragmentation of Benevento

name: Principality of Salerno
note: Lombard principality in southern Italy until Norman conquest

name: Arab Emirate of Bari
note: Short-lived Aghlabid-linked emirate on Adriatic coast

name: Emirate of Sicily
note: Aghlabid then Kalbid rule over Sicily with Palermo as capital

name: Norman County of Apulia and Calabria
note: Hauteville Norman county preceding the Kingdom of Sicily

name: Norman Kingdom of Sicily
note: Norman kingdom uniting Sicily and southern Italian mainland under Roger II

name: Hohenstaufen Kingdom of Sicily
note: Staufen rule of Sicily/southern Italy under Henry VI and Frederick II

name: Hohenstaufen Imperial Italy
note: Staufen dynasty's Italian holdings centered on Frederick II (distinct from Sicily kingdom)

name: Angevin Kingdom of Naples
note: Capetian House of Anjou rule of mainland southern Italy after Sicilian Vespers split

name: Aragonese Kingdom of Sicily
note: Crown of Aragon rule of Sicily following the Sicilian Vespers

name: Kingdom of Sicily
note: Long-running Sicilian kingdom (Aragonese to Spanish to Bourbon) prior to merger into Two Sicilies

name: Kingdom of Naples
note: Mainland southern Italian kingdom (Angevin to Aragonese to Bourbon to Murat)

name: Kingdom of the Two Sicilies
note: Bourbon union of Naples and Sicily until annexation by Sardinia-Piedmont

name: March of Tuscany
note: Carolingian/Imperial march governing Tuscany until fragmentation into communes

name: Republic of Florence
note: Florentine commune and Medici-led republic until transition to duchy

name: Grand Duchy of Tuscany
note: Medici then Habsburg-Lorraine grand duchy until annexation to Sardinia

name: Duchy of Milan
note: Visconti then Sforza duchy in Lombardy before Habsburg annexation

name: Spanish Duchy of Milan
note: Spanish Habsburg possession of Milan until War of Spanish Succession

name: Republic of Genoa
note: Maritime oligarchic republic in Liguria until Napoleonic abolition

name: County and Duchy of Savoy
note: Savoyard realm straddling Alps before elevation to Kingdom of Sardinia

name: Kingdom of Sardinia
note: Aragonese/Savoyard kingdom; Savoy-Piedmont core of Risorgimento after 1718

name: Giudicati of Sardinia
note: Four independent Sardinian kingdoms (Torres / Gallura / Arborea / Cagliari)

name: Giudicato of Torres
note: One of four Sardinian giudicati during medieval Sardinian independence

name: Aragonese Sardinia
note: Crown of Aragon (then Spanish) rule of Sardinia until cession to Savoy

name: Pisan County of Cagliari
note: Pisan-controlled southern Sardinian county until Aragonese conquest

name: Cisalpine / Italian Republic
note: Napoleonic client republic in northern Italy preceding Kingdom of Italy

name: Italian Social Republic
note: Axis-aligned rump state (RSI) in northern Italy under Mussolini after Allied landings

name: Fascist Italy
note: Mussolini-era Italian kingdom under the National Fascist Party

name: Italian Republic
note: Post-war Italian parliamentary republic succeeding the Kingdom of Italy

name: Aragonese Kingdom of Naples
note: Aragonese (Trastámara) rule of Naples under Alfonso V and successors until French conquest
